content:
# Hebrews Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "the elementary doctrine of Christ" vs. "the basic teachings about Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the word of the beginning of Christ" | ton tēs archēs tou Christou logon |
| LOGOS | "the elementary doctrine of Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the basic teachings about Christ again and again" | Adds "again and again"; pluralizes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "again and again" — not in the Greek (the author says "not laying again," not "not going over again and again"). The rhetorical intensification is PNEUMA's addition.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.6 — "crucifying once again the Son of God to their own harm" vs. "by rejecting the Son of God, they themselves are nailing him to the cross once again"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they are crucifying again for themselves the Son of God and putting him to open shame" | anastaurountas heautois ton huion tou theou kai paradeigmatizontas |
| LOGOS | "they are crucifying once again the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "by rejecting the Son of God, they themselves are nailing him to the cross once again and holding him up to public shame" | Adds "by rejecting"; "nailing him to the cross" for anastaurountas |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "by rejecting the Son of God" as an interpretive cause — the author does not state the reason for the re-crucifixion, only the action. "Nailing him to the cross" expands the single verb anastaurountas ("re-crucifying") into a graphic description. The author's compressed language is more powerful precisely because it is compressed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds cause and expands the verb.

---

### v.9 — "we feel sure of better things" vs. "we really don't believe it applies to you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we are persuaded concerning you, beloved, of the better things" | pepeismetha de peri hymōn... ta kreissona |
| LOGOS | "yet in your case, beloved, we feel sure of better things — things that belong to salvation" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "we really don't believe it applies to you. We are confident that you are meant for better things" | "Don't believe it applies to you" — restructures; "meant for" adds |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says "we are persuaded concerning you of better things" — a positive assertion of confidence. PNEUMA restructures into a negative ("we don't believe it applies to you") followed by a positive. The author's approach is positive throughout — he doesn't distance the readers from the warning, he expresses confidence in them.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures positive into negative-then-positive.

---

### v.14 — "Surely blessing I will bless you and multiplying I will multiply you" vs. "I will certainly bless you, and I will multiply your descendants beyond number"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Surely blessing I will bless you and multiplying I will multiply you" | ē mēn eulogōn eulogēsō se kai plēthynōn plēthynō se |
| LOGOS | "Surely I will bless you and multiply you" | Smooths the Hebraism |
| PNEUMA | "I will certainly bless you, and I will multiply your descendants beyond number" | Adds "your descendants beyond number" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "your descendants beyond number" — the Genesis context includes descendants, but the author of Hebrews quotes only the blessing and multiplication without specifying descendants. The quotation is deliberately compressed — the author's point is the oath's certainty, not its content.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds content from Genesis not quoted here.

---

### v.19 — "a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul" vs. "a strong and trustworthy anchor for our souls"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "an anchor of the soul, both sure and firm" | ankuran tēs psychēs asphalē te kai bebaian |
| LOGOS | "a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a strong and trustworthy anchor for our souls" | "Strong" for asphalē; "trustworthy" for bebaian; pluralizes "soul" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. asphalēs means "secure/sure" (literally "unfailing") — not "strong." bebaios means "firm/steadfast" — not "trustworthy." The adjectives describe the anchor's reliability, not its strength or moral quality. PNEUMA shifts from security to strength/trust.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds content | 2 | "Again and again" (v.1), "descendants beyond number" (v.14) |
| PNEUMA restructures | 2 | Negative-then-positive (v.9), adds cause to v.6 |
| PNEUMA shifts adjective meanings | 1 | "Strong/trustworthy" for "sure/steadfast" (v.19) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 6's disagreements are moderate. The most notable is v.6 — PNEUMA adds "by rejecting the Son of God" as an interpretive cause for the re-crucifixion that the author does not state. The author's warning is all the more powerful for being unexplained: those who fall away are crucifying the Son of God again — period. Adding a cause softens the blunt force of the statement.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
